competitor_patterns: Top-ranking pages (OutdoorGearLab, Bikerumor) have extremely high word counts (16,000–24,000 words) with comprehensive multi-category coverage (MTB, road, value, budget) in a single pillar article — far exceeding our current 1,100–3,400 word posts

All top-3 competitors use structured comparison tables with spec rows (weight, lens type, price, UV rating) alongside star ratings and editorial scores — making content scannable and triggering Google rich results
Every top-ranking page includes real-world test context ('we tested 18 pairs', 'tested on real rides') with reviewer bylines and dates, establishing E-E-A-T signals our posts currently lack
Competitor H2 structures segment by use case (MTB vs road vs budget vs women's) and by specific product model, capturing a wide net of long-tail navigational + informational queries in one URL

FAQ sections are universally present in top-3 results, and evil eye (ranking #1 for interchangeable lens glasses) leads with the core technical differentiator (interchangeable lenses) in both the title tag and the first H2 — a direct match to high-intent search intent we are not exploiting for our StradaPro
our_gaps: Word count is critically low: our longest post is 3,357 words vs competitor averages of 16,000–24,000 words — Google's ranking signals for competitive head terms require substantially more topical depth and entity coverage

We have no comparison tables with competitor spec data (weight, price, lens options, UV rating side-by-side) — this is a standard feature of every top-ranking page and a key trigger for featured snippets and AI Overviews

No visible author byline, testing credentials, or E-E-A-T signals on our posts — top competitors explicitly name reviewers and describe real-world test conditions, which Google increasingly weights for YMYL-adjacent product content

Our posts are product-angle-first rather than use-case-first — we lead with StradaPro rather than segmenting by rider type (road racer, commuter, MTB, women's, prescription wearers), missing the long-tail query clusters competitors capture with sub-sections

Zero external backlink-bait elements: no original data, no downloadable buying checklist, no embedded video, no shareable infographic — competitors' high domain authority pages include these to attract links, while our content has no natural link magnet
keyword_opportunities: best interchangeable lens cycling sunglasses 2026 — evil eye ranks #1, we have the exact feature (VellutoPuro + VellutoVisione click-in system) but no dedicated optimised post targeting this exact phrase

lightweight cycling glasses under 30g — our 25g frame is a hard differentiator; no current post targets weight as the primary search intent, yet weight is a documented purchase trigger for road cyclists

anti fog cycling glasses for climbing — our existing anti-fog post covers the broad topic but not the specific climb-context query where fogging is the #1 complaint; this long-tail has clear commercial intent
cycling glasses 30 day trial — our risk-free trial is a unique conversion hook with near-zero competition for the exact phrase; targets bottom-of-funnel searchers comparing purchase risk
best road bike glasses for narrow faces — fit + adjustable nose pad content targets a high-frustration, underserved segment that competitors mention briefly but never dedicate a full post to
next_post_guidelines: Write a minimum of 6,000 words for any post targeting a head keyword ('best cycling glasses', 'interchangeable lens cycling glasses') — structure it as a true pillar page with H2 sections per use case (road, MTB, commute, women's, budget) to capture the full query cluster in one URL

Open every post with a concrete test statement: name the specific rides, conditions, and duration used to evaluate the product — e.g. 'We rode the StradaPro across 14 sessions including a 1,200m climb in 28°C heat and a wet spring classic' — to build E-E-A-T credibility from the first paragraph

Include at least one comparison table per post with ≥5 competitor products listed by spec (weight in grams, lens swap system yes/no, UV400 yes/no, anti-fog system yes/no, price in EUR, trial period) — position StradaPro's 25g / click-in lenses / 30-day trial as the column that wins most rows

Add a named author bio block at the bottom of each post with cycling credentials, ride distance logged, and a headshot — this directly addresses Google's E-E-A-T requirements and differentiates from thin affiliate content

Target the interchangeable lens angle aggressively in the next post: the query 'interchangeable lens cycling glasses' has a #1 competitor (evil eye) with a transactional page, not an editorial guide — a 4,000+ word editorial guide with specs, how-to lens swap instructions, and a comparison table can outrank a product category page

Structure FAQ sections with exact question phrasing pulled from 'People Also Ask' for each target keyword — minimum 6 FAQ items per post, using schema markup — to increase chances of appearing in Google AI Overviews and PAA boxes

For every post, add a clear internal link cluster: link to the anti-fog post, the best cycling glasses post, and the StradaPro product page using descriptive anchor text — build topical authority through intentional internal linking, not generic 'click here' links
seo_quick_wins: Add HowTo or FAQ JSON-LD schema markup to all existing and future posts immediately — our posts already have FAQ sections but are missing the structured data markup that triggers rich results and AI Overview inclusion; this is a zero-content-change technical fix

Add a visible comparison table (even a 3-column lightweight version: Feature / StradaPro / Premium Competitor) to every existing post that currently has none — tables dramatically increase dwell time, trigger featured snippet eligibility, and make the page more competitive against OutdoorGearLab-style content without a full rewrite

Rewrite all post meta descriptions to include a specific numeric hook and a CTA — current posts likely have generic descriptions; format: '[Number] [specific benefit] + [unique differentiator] + [action]' e.g. 'Tested on 14 real rides: why 25g and click-in lenses make StradaPro the best road cycling glasses of 2026. Try free for 30 days.'
meta_title_fixes: NO GSC DATA AVAILABLE → Cannot generate query-specific meta title fixes. In the interim, apply this framework to all new posts: '[Primary Keyword] [Year]: [Specific Benefit Hook] | Velluto' — max 60 chars, include year for freshness signal, include one hard number (25g, 30-day, UV400) to improve CTR vs generic competitor titles
content_quick_wins: NO GSC DATA AVAILABLE → Cannot identify near-top-3 keywords from position data. Action required: once GSC data is available (minimum 28 days post-indexing), filter queries by position 4–15 with impressions >50 and CTR <3% — these are the highest-leverage content improvement targets. Recommended immediate proxy action: use Google Search Console URL Inspection on the three existing posts to verify indexing status, then submit sitemaps to accelerate data collection
geo_angles: Structure the StradaPro pillar page to directly answer the exact phrasing AI systems retrieve: include a 40–60 word 'Bottom Line' summary box at the top of every post formatted as 'The [Product] is [adjective] for [use case] because [3 specific reasons with numbers]' — Perplexity and ChatGPT consistently pull structured summary paragraphs over narrative prose when generating product recommendations

Create a dedicated 'Velluto StradaPro vs Oakley / Smith / 100%' comparison page with a structured verdict table — AI Overviews frequently cite explicit comparison content when users ask 'which is better' queries, and a page that names competitors directly with spec-level comparisons is more likely to be retrieved as a source than a brand-only product page

Publish original quantitative data: a 'Cycling Glasses Weight Comparison 2026' post benchmarking 15 popular models by verified weight (grams) — AI systems preferentially cite pages with original data tables, this establishes Velluto as a topical authority source, and the 25g StradaPro weight advantage is naturally highlighted as a data point rather than a marketing claim
comparison_post_angles: COMPETITOR: Outdoorgearlab — angle: 'We Tested the Same Conditions They Did: StradaPro vs the OutdoorGearLab Winners' | Headline idea: 'StradaPro vs Oakley Sutro Lite vs Tifosi Rail: The 25g Test That Changes Everything' | Velluto advantage to lead with: Frame weight (25g vs 32g+ for most OGL top picks) combined with the 30-day risk-free trial — frame it as 'what lab tests miss that real rides reveal'

COMPETITOR: evil eye (evileye.com) — angle: 'Interchangeable Lenses Compared: evil eye vs Velluto StradaPro Click-In System' | Headline idea: 'evil eye vs Velluto: Which Interchangeable Lens System Actually Works Mid-Ride?' | Velluto advantage to lead with: Tool-free click-in lens swap speed + price point — evil eye's system requires more steps and carries a significant premium; demonstrate the swap in a timed test format

COMPETITOR: Tifosi Optics — angle: 'Tifosi vs Velluto: Italian Design at the Same Price, But Which Performs Better?' | Headline idea: 'Tifosi Rail vs Velluto StradaPro: The Anti-Fog Climb Test (1,200m, 28°C)' | Velluto advantage to lead with: Built-in anti-fog system vs Tifosi's coating-dependent approach — use the climb test as the editorial hook since fogging on climbs is Tifosi's most common negative review complaint
